title: Closing Over/Underapplied Overhead
event: At year-end, Reyes Co.'s Factory Overhead account shows actual overhead and applied overhead that differ by $7,000. Prepare the entry to close the difference to Cost of Goods Sold.

entries.label: Applied too little — jobs were undercosted

lines.label: catch-up
lines.why: Jobs absorbed 7,000 less than reality — COGS takes the catch-up charge.
lines.trap: Underapplied INCREASES COGS. The word 'under' describes the applying, not the fix.

lines.label: account zeroed
lines.why: Clears the debit balance left in the clearing account.
lines.trap: After closing, Factory Overhead is exactly zero — it never survives to the statements.

entries.label: Applied too much — jobs were overcosted

lines.label: account zeroed
lines.why: Clears the credit balance — more was applied than actually incurred.
lines.trap: A credit balance in the clearing account means OVERapplied.

lines.label: give-back
lines.why: Jobs carried 7,000 of overhead that never happened — COGS gets the refund.
lines.trap: Overapplied DECREASES COGS. Direction errors here flip net income by double the amount.

memorize.body: Underapplied → COGS Up. Overapplied → COGS dOwn.
Balance check: whichever side of Factory Overhead is bigger tells you the direction — leftover debits = underapplied.

questions.prompt: Actual overhead $205,000, applied $198,000. Effect of the closing entry on COGS = ?

distractors.feedback: Direction flipped. Actual exceeded applied, so jobs were UNDERcosted — COGS must go UP 7,000.
The clearing account can't keep a balance — the 7,000 gap must close to COGS (or be prorated).

build.scaffold: Find which side of Factory Overhead is heavier, zero it, and send the difference to COGS.
